sentences.text: In Funfteichen, I was there a year and a few months.
Except that there was a lot of beating going on there, too.
This-- this Sturmbannfuhrer Schamal was a sadist.
He used to-- he used to grab people by the shirt and-- and pull towards you, and then let it loose and used to hit you with the elbows in your face.
So that Schamal knocked four teeth in my mouth, knocked out four teeth.
Also, he would take a shovel, like a spade, and he would hit you on-- in the back of your calf, legs.

I was doing something, I was carrying something, and I did not-- you had to take off your hat and-- like a salute and look-- look-- look him in-- in the eyes-- and I was carrying something in camp and I-- could have been a bale of hay or something.

I was doing a special commander work, and-- and-- and every time, so he just get up, so one time when he hit me like that with the right elbow, and then when I got up he did the same thing with the left elbow.
And I was bleeding.
And he says, this is better than a bullet, because a bullet costs money.
He mumbled in-- to me in German, and I understand German, so I know exactly what he was talking.
And another time I was doing some work and he hit me in the back of my calves, on the leg.
And he used to-- that-- that hurts quite a bit.
And I've seen-- I was lucky enough-- because I've seen him where he would hit people with a shovel, on your throat, or on your head.
And when you fell down, he would take the shovel and place it across your neck and step on it on both sides.
This I have something new for me, a new title, and I've seen him do this quite a few times.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was the Report Fuhrer and a Sturmbannfuhrer in charge of the inner-- inner-- in the-- like the fuhrer of the concentration camp inside-- not for daily operations.

And he-- he would-- he would walk away from it, and it wouldn't even like-- wouldn't phase him, it wouldn't bother him, and maybe look for another, the next person to receive the same kind of a treatment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was a daily routine concentration camp, a working concentration camp.
Food was always a problem, never enough food.
Cold was a problem, and the conditions were at least a little better than Auschwitz.
And we-- I was there for a year and a few months.
Oh, because my friends always were there to-- to help me out, because I-- I would not go to the infirmary.
I-- at one time I was terribly sick, and I was in-- in Funfteichen.
I think I must have had typhoid fever, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there was a doctor from my hometown that was working in the infirmary. but I went to see him.
But not as a-- I didn't want to stay there because they constantly used to take the people and clean out the infirmary, and send them to--
To the gas chamber, but we did not-- I mean, to the-- to the crematoriums, but in Funfteichen, they would just transport their bodies somewheres to another camp, maybe Gross-Rosen.
Because maybe-- there were-- there were a lot of camps in this area because this was in the-- in the Silesian part of Germany.
So--
Yeah, and he said to me, this pill-- is-- this is the only pill I have left, and these-- these are hard to come by and I'm gonna give you this pill.
And this will make you completely healthy.
I think it was power of suggestion.
I wanted so hard to get well that I-- I recouped from it.
I-- I also had like a little looking glass, a mirror.
I don't know where I got it, but I used to keep it under my bed.
And I used to look at it, and I must have been delirious from the temperature, you know, the high temperature, and it seemed to me like my face is getting smaller every time I looked at it.
But I was very fortunate because maybe I-- I heal fast and that kept me alive, because they constantly transported people out of the camp-- dead bodies, not people.
Because this camp did not have a crematorium.

But the smell was always prevalent no matter what, because the conditions and the sanitary facilities were very minimum, and limited, and-- so-- but it was like, not-- not-- not enough to live and not enough to die.
This camp we had to evacuate because I think that we-- they were-- the Russian-- were moving closer, and they were always concerned to leave live prisoners wherever they, so they always shipped us.
So they-- from there, we went on a death march from Funfteichen.
It was cold, was winter time.
And we were marching-- took us quite a while to get there-- I don't if it was a week, 10 days, or two weeks.
That must have been like late 1940-- late 1943--
'40-- late 1944, right.
Late 1944.
People, we-- we were marching all day, every day.
Every day, we slept wherever we were happened to be close someplace where there was a farm so that the SS troopers could get something.
So we lost a lot of people on this march because the exposure to the cold weather and the-- and the food was not sufficient.
And the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no sanitary facilities.
I had, in one march I think I had those dutch shoes, those--
Clogs.
Yeah, I don't remember exactly on which one, maybe this was the one or maybe the one-- because I was on two death marches which occurred later.
And we-- many people, just fell.
I mean, they-- they couldn't take it anymore.

So first, they got weak, so then we went in the back of the ones that were stronger, younger, and we took those in the-- from the back and brought them back front, and give-- so other people could hold on to them, you know, two other people and walk with them.
But-- so they got tired, you know, and-- and everyone was afraid to get tired because there was a-- an officer.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If there was a lower ranking officer-- must have been like a Sturmbannfuhrer-- he was on a motorcycle in the back of the column, and he had a-- a mauser in a wooden holster with a quite a long pistol.
And-- and he would just pull up his motorcycle, and take out his pistol and shoot right through the head in one bullet for everyone that fell out.

Well, it-- it got to the point where the SS were getting tired because they were-- they already-- they took all these young people and sent them to the front to fight the Russians because they were losing a lot of people then.
So they-- they-- he told the SS, if-- if you are not gonna keep up, I'm gonna do the same thing to you-- shoot you like a dog.
So they--
We wound up from-- in Gross-Rosen.
Gross-Rosen was a collector's camp.
Was-- we-- we came to Gross-Rosen and we--
During the march, however, to Gross-Rosen, the food was always a problem, so sometimes we would be able to find something because we always stopped where there was a farm, as I said before.

And-- and I-- I remember, I found some eggs that-- and I brought-- an incident which is very important and I-- because we were like a group of seven, which we band together just to stay alive because we were marching with Cossacks, and we were marching with the gypsies, and we were marching with-- you-- you name-- you name it, with Poles.
And this is big bully, the Pollack, said to me, give me the eggs, I said no, I'm not going to give it to you, so he gave me a punch in the nose, and I was bleeding all over.
Well, one of-- we were like a group of seven-- one of the men, you know, he said, why do you pick on this little guy?
So, he said, if you want to fight somebody, why don't you fight me?
He says, I'll be happy to fight you because it takes seven Jews to carry a bag of feathers, and you think you're going to whip me?
Well, he beat the living daylight out of this guy, and he walked away.
And we-- and we shared-- this group always, whoever found something, or was able to get a hold of something, we all shared.
There were seven of us.

Three brothers, my present brother-in-law-- which he married my sister after the war, and my cousin, who we went from one camp to another, Jack Rosenbaum so-- and a few other people-- one other person, actually, and three-- and the three brothers who were butchers so they were hunky guys, and-- and that's why we always-- we always stayed together and helped each other.
You said that you had lost Jack in the ghetto in Sosnowitz, I think, or--
Yeah, but then we were reunited again in Auschwitz.
Yeah, we were reunited in Auschwitz, and we-- from Auschwitz we went to Funfteichen.
And we were together on a death march, and-- and-- we must have lost maybe close to half the people that we started out of from Funfteichen to Gross-Rosen.
And Gross-Rosen, we were taken into barracks that didn't have any baths.
You-- there was no room because they-- they collected all these people to Gross-Rosen, the collector's camp.
And so we were-- we was sitting like a herring.
And you had to sit.
You-- you spread-- you had to spread your legs, and the next guy would sit in front of you.
People were sitting behind you, and if you had to go to the bathroom, the whole row would have to stand up because there was no room-- there were like wall-to-wall people.
And the-- and there were terrible conditions.
A lot of people got sick.
And there was diseases.
Well, we-- we were not-- we-- you did not-- you were not able to lie down to sleep.
You had to sit like packed in herring, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the facilities were limited.
There were not enough bathrooms and not enough-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a shortage of everything because they-- they bunched in so many people to this camp.
So we-- but one of the group, and one of the brothers from-- from the butchers, worked in the kitchen.
They got to-- they were assigned to the kitchen and cooks, and they were able to bring some food.
And that was helping out the rest of it.
But we had to be very careful because people, if they knew that you had food on you, the Russians or the gypsies, they would stab you at night and take it away from you.
So-- so I used to walk around and-- we never worked in-- in this camp-- this was only a camp to be redistributed or to be whatever they-- they were planning to do.
The-- How long were you in Gross-Rosen?
Not too long, not too long at all, a few weeks.
From Gross-Rosen, we-- we were sent on an open rail, you know, rail-- railroad cars-- cattle-- real cattle cars and no roof.

And-- and we-- we was sent to a few camps, I think, where-- and they used to pull us over on the side, and we used to stand there for a day or two because their wehrmacht had the first priority to-- to go through.
We were-- we were sent somewheres to a camp where they didn't have the room for us, and we were parked outside of Dresden.
And it was raining, it was terrible weather, and cold, and we were parked-- we was-- put there-- and we stood there for quite a while.

Maybe like 100 yards away from us, there was another train with American prisoners, which they were-- they at least had food, Red Cross, and-- and they were treated more human like, at least, till that point.
But they were bombing Dresden at that time, and there was a direct hit on that transport of American prisoners.
And many people were killed, and some of them that survived tried to run away so they could save their life.
And-- and we-- the wehrmacht-- I think it was wehrmacht-- they set up machine guns under the train and mowed them all down.
It was a horrible sight to watch because the Americans were our final solution to stay alive, so we were-- that was a sad moment for the whole transport.
Then we finally-- they shuffled us around, and we finally arrived at this camp called Hersbruck.
That's not far from Nuremberg.

In Hersbruck, they were building-- we were building tunnels, big tunnels, part of them were already tiled, you know, like a-- what-- what is to be a underground factory for the Messerschmitt jet plane, because everything was-- they bombed everything else, so they-- that was what they wanted to do.
So we were-- that was a terrible camp, that Hersbruck, because we walked for quite a while-- at least one hour or two to the camp to the work site.
Walked, right.
And the same thing in the evening.
And then when we come home in the evening, we had to clean up the camp, police the area, and-- and received very little sleep there.
So people working like zombies and resigned to the fact that they're going-- not going to make-- make it.
This was a terrible sight because every time we walked, and people would run-- start running so they can be shot, you know, so-- because they knew they will be shot.
Or, if there was a train that would come by, they would run and throw themselves in front of the train or trucks.

So there was a constant-- there was a very morbid type of daily life because-- and-- and that's a place where I was beaten severely because I didn't-- I was so tired because sleep was not enough sleep.

By the time you got up in the morning, and you cleaned up everything, and you worked an hour-- at least an hour-- to work, and after work-- the building, digging the tunnels-- and the-- and walk-- walk back to the camp, and-- so-- and then you had to clean up the camp, and do whatever they told you to.
And this was a very-- probably not much known about this camp.
It was a horrible camp because this camp also didn't have crematoriums, but it had an open pit where they burned the bodies.
And that was probably the most horrible smell because there was like an open fire.
And at night, they would take commanders.
They would say you, you, and you, and to take the bodies and carry him there and throw him into the pit.
Many times the SS troopers would take their rifle butts and push you in, and so there were screams.
People were burned alive, because they would hit him on the head and push him in or stab him, and-- and throw him into the fire.
So there was always, every night, there was like terrible cries, and yelling, and moaning, and-- and-- and you could really see the fire.
You know.
No, no.
Maybe I wasn't big enough.
Maybe I was lucky that they didn't pick me.
Real tired, yeah, and I was really received some severe beating.
I was beaten with a--
--whip on my rear, but that was already-- that was easy, that was easier.
I was beaten up pretty badly, which also took me a while to get over it.
In Auschwitz.
Right, but in-- but in Funfteichen, I was beaten a different way.
I was hit with a shovel, and I was hit with the-- with the--
--with the elbows in my face.
Hersbruck.
Again, right.
Right, and that was-- I was lucky that this was the rear end, because in Hersbruck, they had different kind of methods which I haven't seen in other camp.

They would-- they would take you into the-- the kapos-- they would send the kapos with you to take you to the bathroom, and they would turn on the hose and stick it in your inside and turn it on, so that you, the body, would explode.
No, in through-- into your mouth and down the-- your trachea.
No.
No one ever survived that.
This was--
So that was, right, because I was.
And I got up real quick, and I got dressed.
And so-- they thought maybe-- and I was also younger-- so they thought maybe I will do some work for a little bit longer, you know, because youth had a lot to do with being alive.
Also, a potluck had also a lot to do with it, because you never knew when you will get hit or with what.
Many time depended where you were at this particular moment for no reason whatsoever, or someone will get ticked off about something, and mainly the SS troopers or the kapos.
The kapos were just as murderous as the SS-- SS troopers.
They carried out a lot of punishments.
So in this camp, we-- I was-- I never-- I never volunteered for anything, but I volunteered always to unload the fruit, and vegetable, and bread and-- for the-- for the SS kitchen, you know.
And I was the youngest, and the smallest.
I used to get under the truck and dig a hole and put the food in.
And maybe recover it the next day.
But after a few times, I realized it's a lost cause because the-- the animals found it first at night.
So in-- in this camp, we were not that long because from-- from Hersbruck, we went on another death march, which was a severe death march-- really very bad because it was during winter time.
And in Bavaria, it's-- it's very, very cold.
So we-- we were taken on a march.
And from there we marched to Dachau.
The-- it was very cold, and it was very limited amount of food-- also a lot of people-- the same thing.
They would fall out, fall, and they would be shot, same-- same kind of a routine as from Gross-Rosen, from Funfteichen to Gross-Rosen, one bullet in the head.
We lost a lot of people.
We also came-- it was-- they-- they were already a little more disorganized because the wehrmacht-- they always took us into the forest if they wanted to rest, not because we wanted to, the SS.
And they already had older people, because all the young people they took to fight both-- now they had to fight the American front as well.
And that was a very sad time, too, because they were telling us, the SS troopers were telling us that the Americans are going to lose the war because your father, Roosevelt, died.
This was during the time when-- and-- and they were-- just to give us that extra coup de grace.
And so they, every day, they would remind us about that.
Well, we took-- they took us to one forest one time, and we were strafed by the American Mustangs.
We lost a lot of people.
I was-- this is the march, I think, where I was wearing the-- the clogs, the wooden clogs, because now I remember I was-- my feet were hurting me so much.

And I was sitting-- sitting behind a tree-- they have those old trees-- and-- and-- and the Mustangs would come down very low, and they would throw the concussion mines, which explode above ground and kill everything in sight.
And the wehrmacht at that time was using horses as well.
They confiscated all the horses wherever they could.

And-- and-- and so I took off a shoe, and I was ready to take off the other shoe, and lo and behold, they came down with those mines and-- and-- and the bullets that were very explosive when they hit-- you know, that they used.
And there were bodies flying all over, and portion of-- portion of bodies, and it was a bloody scene.
And then they-- some of the mines hit the horses, and some of the wehrmacht because we were very close by, so we--
OK.
